claim: Every SETTLED/ENFORCED requirement's enforced_by entries shall resolve to a concrete enforcer — a registered check_* invariant or a real top-level Test* function under internal/**/*_test.go — via the shared enforcer-resolution logic.
why: Wave 1 (seed coherence audit): check_enforced_names_invariant (internal/invariants/enforcement.go) only checks that enforced_by is NON-EMPTY for an ENFORCED requirement -- a real typo in an entry (a renamed test, a stale check_* name, a leftover pytest-style node-id like test_x.py::test_y from an earlier implementation) passes that check silently and is only discovered when someone runs `hotam gate` by hand and it fails closed to the full suite. That is an honest but SILENT tax on the T1 tiered gate (R-verify-closure-per-action): the graph claims a targeted enforcer exists when it does not, and the resolver never sees this as debt. check_enforced_by_resolvable (internal/invariants/enforcement.go) makes the debt visible: it resolves every check_* entry against the All registry (graph-local, no filesystem), and resolves every other entry against the set of real top-level Test* function names under internal/**/*_test.go via gate.TestFuncNames (internal/gate/gate.go walkTestFuncs) — the SAME scan internal/gate uses for targeted test selection — firing a Violation naming the exact unresolvable string. This is the regression guard for the wave-2 rebound that replaced 157 broken pytest-style enforced_by references with real Test*/check_* names. The companion check_enforced_by_test_has_teeth is an HONEST NO-OP: an AST body-scan that confirms a bare test_* is not an empty stub does not translate to a static graph-local check — a toothless test function either fails to compile or passes vacuously only until its invariant regresses (at which point `go test` catches it directly), so there is no need (nor graph-local way) to reproduce the historical AST scan. Resolvability (check_enforced_by_resolvable) and non-emptiness (check_enforced_names_invariant) remain load-bearing.
